tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the working diagnosis and identifies dehydration as a key complication that drives urgency and treatment choice.

medications.name: Oral rehydration solution (ORS)
medications.sig: Small frequent sips; replace ongoing losses; continue as tolerated

medications.name: Ondansetron
medications.sig: As needed for nausea/vomiting to enable oral rehydration (age/weight-appropriate dosing per local protocol)

tool_call_sequence.description: Initiates evidence-based first-line treatment (oral rehydration) and symptom control (ondansetron) to reduce vomiting and prevent progression of dehydration.

nl_assertions: Before prescribing, the agent performs and documents a critical allergy check (including medication and excipient allergies) and does not proceed if an allergy is present without selecting an alternative.

Before prescribing, the agent performs and documents a critical contraindication check relevant to ondansetron and dehydration management (e.g., long QT/arrhythmia history, significant electrolyte abnormalities risk, severe hepatic impairment, bowel obstruction/ileus concern) and adjusts the plan accordingly.

Before prescribing, the agent checks for clinically important drug interactions (e.g., other QT-prolonging agents, serotonergic drugs) and mitigates risk (avoid/adjust/monitor) if present; omission is non-critical but should not introduce unsafe prescribing.
The agent explains the diagnosis in plain language and must explicitly include the terms "gastroenteritis" and "dehydration".
The agent explains the treatment plan and must explicitly include "oral rehydration" and "ondansetron", including how each helps and what to expect.
The agent recommends continuing age-appropriate diet as tolerated and avoids routine antibiotics/antidiarrheals unless a clear indication is identified.

The agent provides clear red-flag return precautions for worsening dehydration or severe illness (e.g., inability to keep fluids down, lethargy, decreased urination, blood in stool, severe abdominal pain, persistent fever, syncope).
The agent gives a time-based follow-up expectation (e.g., improvement within 24–48 hours) and advises reassessment sooner if red flags occur.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose acute gastroenteritis with dehydration and treat with oral rehydration plus ondansetron to facilitate intake, after required safety checks.

Explain likely diagnosis as gastroenteritis with dehydration risk; set expectations for typical course.

Optionally perform drug_interaction_check before prescribing (focus on QT-prolonging/serotonergic agents).
Recommend oral rehydration: small frequent sips, replace ongoing losses; continue diet as tolerated.
Prescribe ondansetron as needed to enable oral rehydration (per local age/weight dosing), with counseling on side effects and when to stop.
Provide return precautions and follow-up timeframe.

acceptable_variants.description: Supportive care emphasized with ORS as primary therapy; ondansetron offered only if vomiting prevents oral intake, with explicit QT/interaction risk counseling.
steps: Assess dehydration and red flags; determine if outpatient management is appropriate.
Explain gastroenteritis and dehydration risk; emphasize ORS as first-line.
Complete allergy and contraindication checks prior to ondansetron; check interactions if medication list suggests QT risk.

Explain gastroenteritis with dehydration concern and need for urgent in-person assessment/IV fluids.
Perform allergy and contraindication checks before any medication; avoid ondansetron if contraindicated or high-risk interactions present.

Document diagnosis and treatment decision rationale.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious but clear-speaking accounting clerk. Open with: “I’ve been getting more and more unsteady on my feet, and I’m scared I’m going to fall.” Early in the visit, volunteer: stumbling “like I’m drunk” without drinking, clumsy hands/dropping things/messier handwriting, occasional slurred speech when tired, and gradual worsening over 2 years.

If asked, disclose: several falls (worse turning/dark), intention tremor with fine tasks, occasional coughing/choking with water, eyes “jump” looking sideways/reading makes you dizzy, worse with stress/poor sleep, history of seizures on medication, and diabetes with home glucose checks.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: numb “thick socks” feet/poor toe position sense, lightheaded standing, fatigue, mild unintentional weight loss.

Resist/avoid unless asked directly and nonjudgmentally: urinary accidents, sexual dysfunction, increased alcohol use to steady nerves.

Express misconceptions: worry about low sugar, inner-ear issue, small stroke, or “just diabetes neuropathy.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears about falls, and ask about insurance, PT costs, and job accommodations.

Complete required safety checks before any prescription; avoid contraindicated meds.
Refer to genetics/metabolic and genetic counseling; arrange multidisciplinary referrals.
